headline: Sugar Land Keeps Rolling, But Harrison's Gem and a Brutal Radiation Loss Are the Real Stories of Week 4
subheadline: The Skeeters are lapping the field, the Bombers are quietly building something real, and Hiroshima deserved better than what the scoreboard gave them.
body:
**0.33 — Kyle Harrison's WHIP over 6.0 innings this week, a performance so dominant it bordered on unfair, anchoring the Albuquerque Bombers' 6–4 upset win over the Abbotsford-Colby Twins.**

## At-a-Glance

| Matchup | Score | Player of the Matchup |
|---------|-------|-----------------------|
| Albuquerque Bombers def. Abbotsford-Colby Twins | 6–4 | Kyle Harrison (0.33 WHIP, 6.0 IP) |
| Athletic Park Dragons def. Hiroshima Radiation | 5–4 | Andrés Giménez (multi-RBI, clutch offense) |
| BaseballPro77 def. Canberra Wombats | 8–2 | Mike Trout (3 HR, 6 R) |
| Benchwarmers def. Buttville Skidmarks | 7–3 | Zach Neto (8 R, 2 HR) |
| General Hospital vs. Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs | 4–4 TIE | Brendan Donovan (.556 OBP) |
| North Correa def. Wisconsin Rapids Reapers | 7–3 | Chris Sale (15 K, 0.75 WHIP, 2 QS) |
| Sugar Land Skeeters def. Riverside Roughnecks | 9–1 | Drake Baldwin (multi-HR, multi-RBI) |

## Thriller of the Week

**Athletic Park Dragons def. Hiroshima Radiation, 5–4**

In a matchup that was far tighter than a single-category margin suggests, the **Athletic Park Dragons** escaped with a 5–4 win that the **Hiroshima Radiation** will spend the week replaying. Andrés Giménez and Will Smith provided the offensive punch that proved just enough, while the Dragons' four saves and holds — an often-overlooked category — served as the quiet difference-maker. The loss stings not just because it was winnable, but because Hiroshima now heads into a brutal matchup against the **Sugar Land Skeeters**, the most punishing draw on the schedule right now.

## The Week's Defining Moment

Through four weeks of the MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball season, it has become increasingly difficult to manufacture a reasonable argument against the **Sugar Land Skeeters** being the class of this league. Their 9–1 dismantling of the **Riverside Roughnecks** this week was not a product of one player getting hot or a rotation running on fumes — it was total baseball, the kind of comprehensive performance that separates contenders from pretenders.

Drake Baldwin, Wilyer Abreu, and Nolan Schanuel each delivered multi-home run, multi-RBI performances, giving Sugar Land eight team home runs and 21 RBI against a Riverside pitching staff that surrendered a 7.91 ERA and 1.76 WHIP. The Skeeters answered on the other side with a 4.13 ERA, solid ratios, and two quality starts — a pitching line that won't make headlines but quietly controlled the matchup from the third inning onward.

What makes this team particularly difficult to game-plan against is the absence of a single point of failure. Three weeks of category dominance, including back-to-back top-two finishes, is the kind of sustained excellence that historically separates genuine championship contenders from hot-start teams that cool in May. At this stage, the **Sugar Land Skeeters** are the undisputed team to beat — and it isn't particularly close.

## Matchup Deep-Dives

### Albuquerque Bombers def. Abbotsford-Colby Twins, 6–4

The **Bombers** won this one on the mound and through plate discipline. Their 2.48 ERA over 29.0 innings was anchored by Harrison's gem, and despite posting zero home runs, they manufactured 19 runs with a .343 OBP. The **Twins** had the better offensive profile on paper — six home runs, 18 RBI, five stolen bases — but their pitching staff allowed the whole thing to unravel, posting a 6.00 ERA with zero quality starts over 39.0 innings. The strategic question for Abbotsford going forward is urgent: Yordan Alvarez can carry an offense, but he cannot also fix a rotation. Merrill Kelly's addition can't come soon enough. Next week, the Bombers will look to build on this momentum while the Twins face a decision point about roster construction.

### Athletic Park Dragons def. Hiroshima Radiation, 5–4

This is covered in full above, but the numbers bear repeating: **Hiroshima** posted a 2.35 ERA, 1.12 WHIP, and 44 strikeouts — superior to the **Dragons** in essentially every pitching metric — and still lost. The Dragons' four saves and holds proved to be the swing category. That Nolan McLean struck out 15 batters in his debut and the team still couldn't close the gap speaks to how unforgiving the category format can be. Regression should favor Hiroshima. The problem is, the next matchup against Sugar Land isn't exactly a soft landing.

### BaseballPro77 def. Canberra Wombats, 8–2

Mike Trout's three-home run, six-run week was the kind of performance that makes you stop scrolling your phone and pay attention. **BaseballPro77** posted eight team home runs, a .382 OBP, and six saves and holds in a comprehensive 8–2 win. Munetaka Murakami added another home run to a lineup that is quietly becoming one of the most dangerous in the league. **Canberra**, meanwhile, produced zero home runs, zero quality starts, and Aaron Nola posted an 8.38 ERA over 9.2 innings. Dylan Cease was the lone bright spot. There are structural concerns in Canberra that a single week's improvement is unlikely to address.

### Benchwarmers def. Buttville Skidmarks, 7–3

Zach Neto's eight-run, two-home run week was spectacular, and the **Benchwarmers** will take the 7–3 win. But make no mistake about what happened here: their rotation posted a 4.50 ERA with zero quality starts and only 26 strikeouts, and they still won because **Buttville's** pitching imploded at a 6.02 ERA. Chase DeLauter's four-home run week is a genuine eye-opener for a prospect of his profile, and Kevin Gausman's 1.50 ERA deserved a better fate. The **Benchwarmers** are fortunate. The **Skidmarks** needed their ace to get more support.

### General Hospital vs. Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs, 4–4 TIE

There are ties in fantasy baseball that feel like moral victories, and this is not one of them — not for either side. **General Hospital** posted a .414 OBP, a 0.95 WHIP, and three quality starts, yet couldn't close. **Wonder Wharf** got Brendan Donovan's .556 OBP and three quality starts of their own, but Shota Imanaga's 9.00 ERA cost them dearly. Both managers will feel this one. General Hospital's pitching depth is legitimately elite; Wonder Wharf's offensive structure remains a concern beyond Donovan's contributions.

### North Correa def. Wisconsin Rapids Reapers, 7–3

Chris Sale was otherworldly. Fifteen strikeouts, a 0.75 WHIP, two quality starts — Sale gave **North Correa** the kind of pitching anchor that winning teams are built around, and the offense answered with 23 runs and a .313 OBP. **Wisconsin Rapids** showed real pitching ability — Bryan Woo posted a 0.83 WHIP, and the team's 39 strikeouts are a legitimate asset — but the offense mustered only one home run and eight RBI. The Reapers are a team whose pitching will keep them competitive; the question is whether the bats can ever match it.

### Sugar Land Skeeters def. Riverside Roughnecks, 9–1

Covered above. The **Roughnecks** are in a difficult position: Shea Langeliers is a real bat, but a 7.91 ERA and 1.76 WHIP from a pitching staff with zero quality starts creates a ceiling problem that one strong hitter cannot resolve. This roster needs structural work before it can be taken seriously in the standings.

## Lucky/Unlucky Team of the Week

**Lucky: Benchwarmers.** A 4.50 ERA, zero quality starts, and 26 strikeouts from their pitching staff is not a winning formula — except when your opponent's pitching staff posts a 6.02 ERA and implodes in four fewer innings. The **Benchwarmers** are 7–3, but if their rotation doesn't improve, luck has a way of running out.

**Unlucky: Hiroshima Radiation.** A 2.35 ERA. A 1.12 WHIP. Forty-four strikeouts. Two quality starts. A superior performance in nearly every pitching metric across the board. And yet the **Radiation** lost, 5–4. This is the kind of result that defines the cruelty of the category format. Hiroshima did almost everything right and walked away with nothing. The standings don't always reflect the truth.

## League Records

General Hospital tied the all-time single-week OBP record of .414 — set by themselves in Week 1 — but no new records were broken this week. It's a notable distinction: the franchise record still stands, but they couldn't quite clear their own bar.

## Manager Spotlight: Albuquerque Bombers

Quietly, methodically, the **Albuquerque Bombers** are building something worth watching. After a difficult stretch to open the season, they've now moved up to seventh in the power rankings on the back of a 6–4 win that showcased what this roster can do when it fires on all cylinders.

Harrison's 0.33 WHIP start is the headline, but the deeper story is roster construction. Christian Yelich's .600 OBP is a genuine on-base weapon, and the waiver additions of Marte and Jung have added real positional depth that gives the manager flexibility in constructing lineup combinations. The pitching staff's 2.48 ERA for the week suggests the rotation might be finding its footing.

The Bombers are not yet threatening the top three, but they look like a team that has made the right calls in the short-term acquisitions market. For a franchise at seventh overall, the upward trajectory is more important than the current position.

## Trade Value Watch

**Kyle Harrison — Hold.** A 0.33 WHIP over six innings is the kind of number that makes opposing managers inquire. Harrison's owners should resist any impulse to sell high; at this point, you hold and let the sample size build. If two or three more starts look like this one, his value will only increase.

**Chase DeLauter — Buy.** Four home runs in a week from a prospect is the sort of performance that gets people's attention. DeLauter's underlying profile suggests the power is real, not a mirage. If he's available or moveable in your league at a reasonable cost, this is the time to acquire him before the market catches up.

**Aaron Nola — Sell.** An 8.38 ERA over 9.2 innings in a single week is alarming for a pitcher of Nola's track record. There's some regression to the mean that will come — he's too good historically to sustain numbers like that — but if you can move him for value while his name still carries weight, the window may be closing.

## Power Rankings

1. **Sugar Land Skeeters** | Third dominant week in four — 9–1 blowout, balanced offense led by Baldwin/Abreu/Schanuel, pitching holds up; no one else is close | ➡ Same
2. **BaseballPro77** | Mike Trout's 3-HR, 6-R explosion headline an 8–2 throttling of Canberra; Murakami adds another HR bat in a deep, dangerous lineup | ⬆ Up
3. **North Correa** | Chris Sale was otherworldly — 15 K, 0.75 WHIP, two quality starts — and the offense delivered 23 runs; this team is peaking at the right time | ⬆ Up
4. **Athletic Park Dragons** | Won a tight 5–4 game they arguably shouldn't have; Giménez and Will Smith showed up big, and 4 NSVH remains an underrated weapon | ⬇ Down
5. **General Hospital** | The tie last week is forgiven — 4–4 with Wonder Wharf is a missed opportunity, but the pitching depth (3 QS, 3.15 ERA, 0.95 WHIP) is legitimately elite | ➡ Same
6. **Hiroshima Radiation** | Nolan McLean's 15-K debut is a revelation; this team outperformed its opponent in nearly every pitching metric and still lost — regression will favor them | ⬆ Up
7. **Albuquerque Bombers** | Kyle Harrison's 0.33 WHIP gem anchored a 6–4 win; Christian Yelich at .600 OBP is back; the waiver haul of Marte and Jung adds real positional depth | ⬆ Up
8. **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** | Shota Imanaga's 9.00 ERA cost them dearly in the tie; Brendan Donovan's .556 OBP keeps the offense afloat but structural hitting concerns persist | ➡ Same
9. **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** | Dropped 7–3 to North Correa but Bryan Woo's 0.83 WHIP and 39 team strikeouts show the pitching is real; offense still the glaring weakness | ⬇ Down
10. **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** | Yordan Alvarez and Jonathan Aranda had monster weeks but the pitching surrendered a 6.00 ERA with zero quality starts — addition of Merrill Kelly is urgent | ⬇ Down
11. **Buttville Skidmarks** | Chase DeLauter's 4-HR week is an eye-opener for a prospect, but Kevin Gausman's 1.50 ERA was wasted in a 7–3 loss; the lineup depth beyond DeLauter is thin | ➡ Same
12. **Benchwarmers** | Zach Neto's 8-run, 2-HR week was spectacular and papered over a rotation with zero quality starts; they won 7–3 but this pitching staff is a ticking clock | ⬆ Up
13. **Riverside Roughnecks** | A 9–1 demolition at the hands of Sugar Land exposes everything — no quality starts, a 7.91 ERA, and a WHIP of 1.76; Shea Langeliers' bat can't carry this alone | ⬇ Down
14. **Canberra Wombats** | Zero home runs, zero quality starts from Aaron Nola (8.38 ERA in 9.2 IP), and only 2 category wins in an 8–2 shellacking; Dylan Cease was the lone bright spot | ➡ Same

## Waiver Wire

No notable waiver moves were reported this week. Given the roster construction questions visible across several teams — most urgently in **Abbotsford-Colby** and **Riverside** — that relative quiet in the transaction wire is itself a story worth monitoring heading into Week 5.
